flags: CORRECTNESS DEFECT: the cut list's 12" back does not reconcile with the 10" bottom and 3/4" sides — neither a between-the-sides reading (10") nor a full-outside reading (11 1/2") produces 12". The rewrite tells the builder to dry-fit and trim the back to the assembled carcass rather than trusting the number.

The tilt angle of the back (and the matching angle on the side tops) is not specified anywhere in the plan — cut list, description, and steps all say only 'angled'. The rewrite describes the cut without asserting a degree.

DERIVED, not proved by the cut list: the bottom and back fit BETWEEN the two sides. run1-box-geometry.mjs returned nothing for this plan; the reading comes from the plan's own instruction that pocket holes go in the bottom and back ONLY so the side faces stay clean, which only works if those two parts are screwed outward into the sides.
Lumber verified with packBoards([10,12,9,9],96) = 1 board, 55 5/8" slack. The single 8 ft 1x3 is ample.

ops.title: Cut the four pieces
ops.body: From the single 1x3, cut one bottom 10" long, one back 12" long, and two sides 9" long — a 1x3's actual size is 3/4" x 2 1/2", so all four pieces share that cross-section and only the lengths differ. That is 40" out of a 96" board, so you have plenty of material to recut a piece if an angle goes wrong. The back and the top edges of the two sides get an angled cut so the back leans instead of standing vertical, and that lean is what tilts magazines forward so their covers face out. Cut the two sides together, or set the saw once and cut them back to back, so their angles match exactly — a mismatch there twists the whole rack.

ops.title: Drill pocket holes
ops.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes in the bottom piece and the back piece only, placing them on the faces that end up hidden inside the rack. The two sides stay clean — they are what you look straight at once the rack is mounted, and a pocket hole there is permanent. Two holes at each end of the bottom and of the back is enough on parts this narrow. Do all of this now, while both pieces are flat and loose; reaching into an assembled rack with a jig is far harder.

ops.title: Attach the bottom
ops.body: Stand the two 9" sides on edge and set the 10" bottom between them, flush with their lower ends, then drive the pocket screws outward from the bottom into the inside face of each side. Glue the joint first — 1x3 pine is narrow, and the screws are doing real work on something that will hang loaded on a wall. Watch that the sides stay square to the bottom as you drive the second screw; the first one can pull the assembly out of square. With the bottom in, the rack has its width and only needs the back to lock it up.

ops.title: Attach the angled back
ops.body: Dry-fit the back against the angled top edges of the sides before you drive anything — it is cut 12" long against a 10" bottom, so check how it actually lands on your assembly and trim it to fit rather than trusting the number. Once it fits, glue it and drive the pocket screws home so it leans back rather than standing upright. That lean is the whole point of the design, and the closed triangle it forms in section is also what stops the rack from racking. Wipe the squeeze-out now, because dried glue will show through paint.

ops.title: Fill, sand, and finish
ops.body: Press wood filler into the exposed pocket holes, let it dry fully, and sand it flush — filler shrinks a little as it dries, so a second thin pass is normal rather than a mistake. Sand every face and ease all the edges and corners, especially the front edges of the sides where hands reach past to pull a magazine out. Paint the rack inside as well as out; the inside faces show whenever it is empty.

ops.title: Mount
ops.body: Hold the rack against the wall where you want it, mark level, and fasten through the back piece into a stud wherever you can reach one — the back is the part with the best chance of landing on solid framing. If you are using adhesive strips instead, hang it low and keep the load light: a rack full of magazines is heavier than it looks, and strips let go all at once rather than gradually. Check it is level once more before the fasteners go fully home.

flags: SYSTEMIC GAP: no step produced the parts. A cut step was inserted at index 0, enumerated straight from the cut list.

Lumber verified with packBoards. 2x6 parts ([83.5 x8, 44 x2], 96") = 9 boards against 9 supplied — exact, no spare. 2x4 parts at 1 1/2" x 3 1/2" ([38 x3, 35.5 x4, 33 x4, 20.25 x8, 64], 96") = 7 boards; the 1 1/2" square X members ([68.625, 34, 34], 96") need 2 more 2x4s ripped down, for 9 total against 9 supplied — also exact. Foot trim ([36, 36], 96") fits the single 1x4.
Arithmetic check that CLOSES: eight 5 1/2" tabletop boards make a 44" wide top, which is exactly the breadboard-end length. Called out in the prose so the builder can self-verify.
The materials list has no 2x2 line, yet the X center support and X arms are 1 1/2" x 1 1/2". They must be ripped from 2x4 stock; the rewrite says so, and the board count above accounts for it.
run1-box-geometry.mjs returned no determination for this plan; no prose asserts which member is captured in any frame.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: From the nine 2x6s, cut eight tabletop boards 83 1/2" long — one per board, since two will not fit on a 96" length — and get both 44" breadboard ends out of the ninth. From the 2x4s cut three long curved leg boards at 38", four mid at 35 1/2", and four short at 33", all measured long point to long point with both ends cut 30 degrees off square and NOT parallel; then eight leg feet at 20 1/4" and the stretcher at 64", which you will trim to your actual leg spacing later. Rip two more 2x4s down to 1 1/2" square for the X center support at 68 5/8" and the two X arms at 34" — the materials list has no 2x2 line, so those come out of 2x4 stock. Cut the two 36" foot trim pieces from the 1x4. All nine 2x6s and all nine 2x4s are consumed with very little to spare, so cut the longest parts out of each board first and take the short pieces from what is left.

ops.title: Build the tabletop
ops.body: Lay the eight 83 1/2" boards face down edge to edge — no breadboard ends yet — and join them with 1 1/2" pocket holes and 2 1/2" pocket hole screws, staggering the holes so they do not all crowd the same line. Eight 5 1/2" boards give a 44" wide top, which is a useful check: that is exactly the breadboard-end length in the cut list. Then clamp a straight board across each end as a guide and run the circular saw down both end edges, truing all eight boards to one length in a single pass — getting eight individual 2x6s to a consistent length by measurement alone is far less reliable. For a smoother top, consider tongue and groove boards.

ops.title: Attach the breadboard ends
ops.body: Measure the trued-up top's actual width — it should land close to 44" — and cut the two breadboard ends to that measured figure rather than to the cut-list number, since the trimming pass just changed it. Attach one to each end with pocket holes and 2 1/2" screws driven from underneath, keeping the top surfaces flush; a breadboard end sitting proud is the first thing a hand finds on a dining table. Clamp across the joint while you drive the screws so nothing creeps.

baseStepTitles: Cut and stack the curved leg boards
Add the second leg base and feet
Attach the leg sets and stretcher

flags: CORRECTNESS DEFECT, fixed in the prose: the source drills the seasoning cavity 'into the top of each blank' while also putting the refill stopper in the BOTTOM. Those cannot both be true — a bottom stopper can only refill a cavity that opens out of the bottom. The rewrite bores the cavity from the bottom face and leaves a solid top for the dispensing holes, which is the only reading in which the plan's own stopper and dispensing-hole steps are both satisfiable.

Cavity depth, wall thickness, dispensing-hole diameter and stopper-hole diameter are not specified anywhere in the plan. The rewrite gives the technique (match the bit to the stopper, test in an offcut, stop short of the top) without inventing a single dimension.
The only cut-list dimension is 2 blanks at 1 1/2" x 1 1/2" x 4"; step 1 previously said only 'cut two hardwood blanks to size'. Now stated.

ops.title: Cut the blanks
ops.body: Cut two blanks 4" long from the 2x2 — a 2x2's actual size is 1 1/2" x 1 1/2", so each shaker is a 1 1/2" square post 4" tall. Square both ends carefully: the bottom has to sit flat on a table and the top is the face the dispensing holes go through. The 8 ft length yields far more blanks than the pair you need, so cut a couple of spares and keep the two with the tightest, most closed grain — open grain traps salt and is harder to keep clean. Mark one blank S and one P on the bottom before you start drilling, because they are identical from the outside once they are done.

ops.title: Drill the seasoning cavity
ops.body: Bore the main cavity into the BOTTOM face of each blank, not the top — the stopper goes in the bottom for refilling, so that is the end the cavity has to open out of, and drilling it from the top would leave no way to fill the shaker. Use a forstner bit for a clean flat-bottomed hole and stop well short of the top face, leaving a solid lid for the dispensing holes to pass through. Clamp the blank and drill against a flat surface so the hole runs parallel to the sides; a 1 1/2" post has very little wall to spare if the bit wanders. Decide the cavity diameter together with your stopper — see the stopper step, because that same opening is what the stopper has to seal.

ops.title: Drill the dispensing holes
ops.body: Drill the small dispensing holes down through the top face until they break into the cavity below — more and smaller for salt, fewer and larger for pepper is a reasonable default, and the pattern itself is yours to choose. Drill from the top face downward so any tear-out lands inside the cavity where nobody will ever see it. Drill one hole first and confirm it actually breaks through before you commit to the rest of the pattern. Do this before any finish goes on, then blow the dust out of the cavity.

ops.title: Drill the stopper hole
ops.body: Because the cavity opens out of the bottom, its mouth is the stopper hole: either bore the whole cavity with a bit matched to your stopper, or bore the cavity smaller and counterbore the bottom to the stopper's diameter. Size it against the actual stopper rather than a number — hold the stopper alongside the bit, then test the fit in a scrap offcut before you touch a finished blank. A forstner bit gives the straight, flat-walled hole a stopper needs to seal against. If you are still choosing, take silicone over cork: it looks a little less traditional but it still comes back out easily on the tenth refill, which cork does not.

ops.title: Sand, finish, and stopper
ops.body: Sand each shaker through progressively finer grits and ease the four long edges and the top corners so it sits comfortably in the hand. Apply the food-safe finish inside and out, including into the cavity, and let it cure fully before any seasoning goes near it — an uncured finish will taint salt. Push the stopper into the bottom of each to close it, and refill through that same hole from then on.

flags: CORRECTNESS DEFECT: the summary and description call for 'a roughly 12" circle', but the cut list's blank is a 2x12 at its actual 11 1/4" width. The largest true circle obtainable is 11 1/4" across, not 12". The rewrite sizes the template to 11 1/4" and says why.

CORRECTNESS DEFECT: the materials line says the board need only be 'at least 1/4" thick', while the cut list specifies a 2x12 blank at 1 1/2" thick. Board thickness is limited from ABOVE by the movement's shaft length, not just from below. The rewrite has the builder check the shaft against the blank and counterbore a recess in the BACK face if needed. No shaft length is asserted — that number is not in the plan.
SYSTEMIC GAP: no step produced the blank. A cut step was inserted at index 0.
The plan lists no saw other than the jigsaw and no tape measure, so the inserted cut step uses only tools from the plan's own list.
Center-hole diameter is not specified (it depends on the movement) — the rewrite matches the bit to the shaft instead of naming a size.

step.title: Cut the blank
step.body: Cut the clock blank 12" long from the board. A 2x12's actual width is 11 1/4", so the blank comes out 11 1/4" x 12" and the largest true circle you can get from it is 11 1/4" across, not the 12" the summary suggests. Cut both ends square — a printed template is going onto this face, and a skewed blank wastes template. Check your movement's shaft against the blank's thickness before you go any further: a 2x12 is 1 1/2" thick, and the shaft has to pass through and still leave thread for the nut on the front.
materials: Board, at least 1/4" thick, cut to 12in

ops.title: Transfer the gear template
ops.body: Print the gear template sized to fit inside the blank — no more than 11 1/4" across, since that is the board's real width. Find and mark the exact center of the blank first, then line the template's center up to that mark so the hands end up centered in the gear. Tape it down all the way around, or use spray adhesive; a template that lifts halfway through a tooth is how a tooth goes wrong. Trace the outline in pencil as well, so you still have a line to follow if the paper tears mid-cut.
materials: Board, at least 1/4" thick, cut to 12in

ops.title: Cut the gear shape
ops.body: Cut the silhouette out with the jigsaw, staying just outside the line and cleaning back to it afterward rather than trying to split the line in one pass. Take each tooth notch as two straight cuts into the corner instead of turning the blade inside the notch — a jigsaw blade twisted in a tight radius wanders and bevels the edge. Drill a relief hole at each inside corner if the notches are tight, and let the blade reach full speed before it touches the wood. Support the offcut as you finish each cut so it does not break away and tear the last tooth off with it.
materials: Board, at least 1/4" thick, cut to 12in

ops.title: Drill the center hole
ops.body: Drill the center hole at the mark you made, sized to your movement's shaft — hold the shaft against the forstner bit to match it rather than guessing a diameter. Back the blank with a scrap so the bit does not blow the face out as it exits. If the blank is thicker than the shaft is long, counterbore a recess on the BACK face with a larger forstner bit until the remaining thickness lets the shaft through with thread to spare; a 2x12 blank is 1 1/2" thick, which is more than most quartz shafts will reach through. Test-fit the movement before you finish anything.
materials: Board, at least 1/4" thick, cut to 12in

ops.title: Sand and finish
ops.body: Sand both faces and every cut edge, working carefully into the tooth notches where the jigsaw left marks — those inside corners are what the eye actually reads as a gear. Ease the sharp corners so the finish does not rub thin on them later. Paint or stain to taste and let it cure fully before the movement goes in. Keep finish out of the center hole and off the back recess, so the movement still seats flat against the wood.

ops.title: Install the mechanism
ops.body: Push the shaft through the center hole from the back, fit the washer and nut on the front, and snug it just enough to hold — over-tightening a quartz movement's plastic case will crack it. Press on the hour hand, then the minute hand, then the second hand, checking that none of them touch each other or the face as they sweep past twelve. Set the time with the movement's own knob rather than by dragging the hands around. Fit the battery and hang it from the movement's built-in hanger.

flags: CORRECTNESS DEFECT: the cut list's back panel is 25 1/2" x 30", which cannot cover a carcass built from 34" top and bottom panels — it is roughly 8 1/2" too narrow. The rewrite has the builder measure the assembled carcass and cut the 1/4" back to fit, and says explicitly that the cut-list number is wrong. Not guessed at a replacement number.

DERIVED, not proved by the script: run1-box-geometry.mjs returned NO determination for this plan, so the sides-run-full reading is arithmetic, not a proof. It is stated in the prose WITH its arithmetic so the builder can check it: 34" top/bottom between two 3/4" sides = 35 1/2" carcass; face frame = 33" rails + two 1 1/2" stiles = 36", the cabinet's named width, overhanging 1/4" per side; and the 33 3/4" shelves then have 1/8" clearance at each end of the 34" opening. Three independent cut-list rows land on the same reading. The back panel row is the only one that does not, and it is flagged above.
SYSTEMIC GAP: no step produced the parts. A cut step was inserted at index 0.

Plywood sufficiency: the 3/4" parts are all 10 1/4" or narrower, so the 4x8 sheet yields four 10 1/4" strips. Lengths pack as 34+34 = 68", 30+30 = 60", 33 3/4+33 3/4 = 67 1/2" — three strips of four. No shortfall.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: Rip the 3/4" sheet into 10 1/4" strips on the table saw — a 48" sheet gives four, and the whole carcass comes out of three of them. Crosscut two sides at 30", two top and bottom pieces at 34", and two shelves at 33 3/4" ripped down to 10" deep so they clear the back panel; take the two 34" pieces off one strip first, since they are the longest and least forgiving parts. Cut the back top support 34" long from the plywood scrap or 1x4. From the 1x2 cut two face frame stiles 30" and two rails 33", and from the 1x3 cut the mid stile 27". Leave the 1/4" back panel uncut for now — its cut-list size does not match this carcass, so it gets measured and cut to the assembled box later.
materials: Plywood scrap or 1x4, for back support

ops.title: Build the carcass
ops.body: Drill pocket holes facing forward along the front edges of the top and bottom, same as the sides, then drill 3/4" pocket holes at each end of both and screw them BETWEEN the two 30" sides with 1 1/4" pocket hole screws. That reading gives a carcass 30" tall and 34" plus two 3/4" sides, or 35 1/2", wide — which is what makes the 36" face frame (33" rails plus two 1 1/2" stiles) overhang 1/4" on each end, and what leaves the 33 3/4" shelves an eighth of an inch of clearance at each end. Every pocket hole stays on the outside of the cabinet; the inside should show no visible joints. Compare the diagonals before the last screws go home — a carcass out of square will not take a square face frame.

ops.title: Attach the back panel
ops.body: Measure the assembled carcass and cut the 1/4" back to that actual size — the cut list's 25 1/2" x 30" back is far too narrow for a 35 1/2" wide cabinet, so trust the box in front of you rather than the number. Confirm the carcass is square first, because nailing a back on locks in whatever shape it is currently holding. The back panel is not structural: it only finishes the inside of the cabinet and must never be used for hanging. Glue it and run 3/4" finish nails around all four edges.

ops.title: Build the face frame
ops.body: Build the frame flat on a bench: the two 30" stiles run the full height, the two 33" rails fit between them, and the 27" mid stile fits between the rails. That is 33" plus two 1 1/2" stiles, or 36" — the width the cabinet is named for. Join it with 3/4" pocket holes, screws and glue, clamping each joint so no member sits proud of its neighbour; a step in a face frame is visible forever. Compare the diagonals before the glue grabs.

ops.title: Attach the face frame
ops.body: Mark 1/4" in from the outside face of each side — that is the overhang the 36" frame gets over the 35 1/2" carcass — and register the frame to those marks so the reveal is even at both ends. Screw it on through the forward-facing pocket holes you drilled into the sides, top and bottom before assembly, with glue on the mating edges. Work from the middle outward and clamp as you go, or the frame will creep as the screws pull.

ops.title: Add the shelves
ops.body: The shelves are 33 3/4" long against a 34" opening, an eighth of an inch of clearance at each end, which is what lets them drop in past the face frame. They are ripped 10" deep rather than the carcass's 10 1/4" so they clear the back panel. Iron or glue edge banding onto the front edge of each one before it goes in — that edge is the only plywood edge anyone sees inside the cabinet. Set them on shelf pins in the holes you drilled at the very start.

ops.title: Hang the doors
ops.body: Bore the hinge cups in the doors and mount the concealed hinges to their own template, then hang the two 17 1/2" x 29" doors on the face frame. Two doors at 17 1/2" come to 35" across a 36" frame, so there is 1" of gap to distribute between the two outer edges and the center — set one door first, then match the second to it rather than trying to split it three ways at once. Concealed hinges adjust in all three directions once mounted, so hang them loose and walk the gaps in afterward instead of relying on drilling accurately.

flags: SYSTEMIC GAP: 27 cut-list rows and no step that produced any of them. A cut step was inserted at index 0. The eight existing assembly steps are already strong and were left byte-identical.

Arithmetic checks that CLOSE and are stated in the new step so the builder can self-verify: sixteen 3 1/2" back slats lay up to 56", exactly the back trim / back skirting / drawer trim length; the 54 1/2" top and bottom shelves are that 56" less two 3/4" sides; and with dividers set 18" in from each side, 54 1/2 - 18 - 18 - 1 1/2 (two divider thicknesses) = 17", exactly the center shelf length.

Lumber verified with packBoards at the plan's own board lengths: 1x4 (44 parts, 192" boards) = 7 boards against 7 supplied, worst slack 1 5/8" — exact, no spare. 1x2 (72" boards) = 6 against 7 supplied. 1x12 (192" boards) = 2 against 3 supplied. No shortfall, but the 1x4 has no margin for a miscut, which the new step says.
run1-box-geometry.mjs returned no determination for this plan; no inserted prose asserts which member is captured in any frame.
The plan lists no miter saw, so the cut step uses the circular saw.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: From the 1x12s cut two sides at 33 1/4", the top and bottom shelf at 54 1/2", two shelf dividers at 25", two side shelves at 18", two center shelves at 17", and the drawer bottom at 50". From the 1x4s cut sixteen back slats at 33 1/4", two back trim at 56", the back skirting at 56", four side trim legs at 21", two short leg supports at 5 1/2" and two long at 7 1/4", two front/back top supports at 59" and two side top supports at 16", ten door slats at 25 1/2", the drawer back at 50", and two drawer sides at 12 1/4". From the 1x2s cut two front side trim at 31 3/4", two center front trim at 27 3/4", two side shelf trim at 16 1/2", two center shelf trim at 17", four door trim at 17 1/2", the drawer trim at 56", and the center top support at 52"; cut the two 4x4 legs at 33 1/4" and the 1x6 drawer front at 53 3/4". Three numbers should agree before you cut anything else: sixteen 3 1/2" slats lay up to 56", which is the back trim length, and the 54 1/2" shelves are that same 56" less the two 3/4" sides. Every part the cut list marks 'measure first' — back trim, center shelves, center shelf trim, drawer trim, back skirting, center top support, door trim, drawer front — should be left long and trimmed to what you have actually built, and cut carefully regardless: all seven 1x4s are consumed with under two inches of slack.

baseStepTitles: Build and set aside the back
Add the back skirting and leg supports
Add the top supports and butcher block

flags: SHORTFALL CLAIM: the 3/4" plywood parts do not fit two 4x8 sheets. Area alone is 8,951 sq in against 9,216 sq in of sheet (97%), and a real strip layout runs out before the doors. Sheet 1 ripped into two 23 1/4" strips takes 4 cabinet panels + the trash shelf + the trash door (84" and 84 1/2" of the 96"). Sheet 2 must then carry the 5th cabinet panel, four 11 1/4" x 34 1/2" open shelf panels, six 11 1/4" x 24 1/2" shelf boards, and two 17 3/8" x 29 1/2" sink-base doors — the panels and boards alone consume it (a 23 1/4" strip and two 11 1/4" strips fill the 48" width, and the shelf boards use the tails). The two sink-base doors have no material left. Not a packBoards case — packBoards is 1-D and cannot model sheet goods; this is a strip-layout argument stated from the plan's own dimensions.

The prose does NOT assert the shortfall as fact; it tells the builder to lay every part out on both sheets before the first cut because the parts consume almost the entire sheet area, which is arithmetic, and to expect to buy door material separately.
SYSTEMIC GAP: no step produced the parts. A cut step was inserted at index 0.

run1-box-geometry.mjs DETERMINATION, used verbatim: 'Drawer sides (20") runs the FULL length and Drawer front/back (12-1/2") sits BETWEEN them — proved by the Drawer bottom at 14" x 20".' The drawer step now states 12 1/2 + 1 1/2 = 14" overall and cross-checks it against the bottom.
The 76 1/2" back panel width is the finished run length and is repeated by the top trim, top trim cap and baseboard — noted in the cut step as a self-check.

step.title: Break down the sheets and cut the trim
step.body: Lay every 3/4" plywood part out on both sheets before the first cut — five cabinet side/shelf panels at 23 1/4" x 34 1/2", the 23 1/4" x 15" trash pull-out shelf, four open shelf panels at 11 1/4" x 34 1/2", six open shelf boards at 11 1/4" x 24 1/2", the 23 1/4" x 15 1/2" trash door and two sink-base doors at 17 3/8" x 29 1/2" together consume almost the entire area of the two sheets, so one careless rip can cost you a whole sheet and you should budget on buying door material separately if your layout comes up short. Rip to width on the table saw and crosscut the long pieces with the circular saw against a clamped guide. Cut the 1/2" back panel 76 1/2" x 34 1/2"; that 76 1/2" is the finished length of the whole run, and the top trim, the top trim cap and the baseboard all repeat it, so those four numbers check each other. From the solid stock cut two side top trim and two side top trim caps at 35 3/4", four front stiles and four mid stiles at 27 1/2", four outer corner stiles at 27 1/2" from the wider 1x3 so the corbels land on solid trim, and the dishwasher end panel trim at 34 1/2". Edge band the front exposed edges of the panels now, while they are still loose and flat.

ops.title: Build and install the drawer
ops.body: The drawer is 20" deep: the two 20" 1x4 sides run the FULL depth and the front and back fit BETWEEN them, so at the listed 12 1/2" the box comes out 14" wide overall — which is exactly the 14" x 20" plywood bottom, and that agreement is your check that you have it the right way round. Install the sides onto the full-extension slides inside the cavity first, then measure the actual gap between them and cut the front and back to that figure rather than to 12 1/2", since slide clearance varies by brand. Join the corners with 3/4" pocket holes and 1 1/4" pocket hole screws. Cut the 1/2" plywood bottom to fit and attach it with glue and 1 1/4" brad nails, setting the box at a height the drawer face will cover.

baseStepTitles: Add the front and side trim
Trim the dishwasher panel and base

flags: SHORTFALL CLAIM, verified with packBoards: the MEDIUM tree's pieces are 9 1/8 + 8 1/8 + four at 4 1/2 + 9 1/2 + 4 3/4 = 49 1/2" of board, against a materials line of '1x board scrap, 4 ft total (medium tree)' = 48". packBoards([9.125,8.125,4.5,4.5,4.5,4.5,9.5,4.75],48) returns 2 boards — it does not fit even before kerf. Small tree: packBoards(...,36) = 1 board, 1 1/8" slack (very tight but sufficient). Large tree: packBoards(...,72) = 1 board, 4 3/4" slack. Stated in the cut step.

SYSTEMIC GAP, partial: the plan had a 'Cut the tree tops' step that produced only 2 of each tree's 8 pieces — the branches, base and trunk were never cut anywhere. Step 0 is replaced with a full cut step covering all three sizes.

The drop from the first row of branches to the second is NOT specified in the source (the first row's 1/2" down from the peak is). The rewrite says so plainly and gives the technique instead of a number.
DERIVED: four branch pieces per tree over two rows implies two per row, one each side of the peak. The cut list gives the quantity but not the arrangement.
run1-box-geometry.mjs returned no determination for this plan; nothing in the rewrite asserts a captured member.

ops.title: Cut all the pieces
ops.body: Cut all eight pieces for each tree before assembling anything — every cut is a simple vertical miter-saw cut at 40 or 25 degrees off square. Small tree: top pieces at 6 3/8" and 5 3/8" (long point to long point, one end 40 degrees and the other 25), four branch pieces at 3 1/2" (ends parallel, both 25 degrees), a base at 5 1/2" (ends NOT parallel, both 25 degrees) and a 2 3/4" trunk. Medium tree: 9 1/8" and 8 1/8" tops, four 4 1/2" branches, a 9 1/2" base and a 4 3/4" trunk — and note that those add up to 49 1/2" of board against the 4 ft the materials list allows, so the medium tree needs a second offcut. Large tree: 12 3/8" and 11 3/8" tops, four 5 1/2" branches, a 13 7/8" base and a 6 3/4" trunk, which does fit the 6 ft allowance with a few inches spare. The two top pieces of each tree differ by exactly 1" — that offset is what forms the peak, so cut them as a pair and label them before they get mixed in with the other sizes.

ops.title: Join the tree tops
ops.body: Bring the two top pieces together at their 40-degree ends so they form the peak, with the shorter one sitting 1" lower at the bottom — that offset is the whole reason the two lengths differ. Glue the joint and drive 1 1/4" brad nails through it, then clamp until the glue sets; clamping this one joint makes a real difference to how clean the top of the finished tree looks. Check that both 25-degree bottom ends still line up on the same plane before you walk away, since they are what the first row of branches registers against.

ops.title: Add the first row of branches
ops.body: Measure down 1/2" from the peak, mark both halves, and glue and brad-nail on the first two branch pieces, one to each side, with their parallel 25-degree ends running the same way as the peak's bottom cuts. Nail through the front into solid material behind so no nail head lands on an edge where it can split out. Wipe the squeeze-out before it skins over — glue that soaks into the grain will show if you ever stain these. If you are building all three sizes, do all three first rows now while the layout is fresh.

ops.title: Add the second row of branches
ops.body: Add the last two branch pieces in a second row below the first, keeping them parallel to it so the silhouette steps outward evenly toward the base. The plan does not specify the drop from the first row to the second, so set a spacing that looks right on the small tree and hold the same proportion on the medium and large ones. Mark both sides from the same reference edge, or the two halves will end up asymmetrical in a way that is very visible on a tree shape. Glue and brad-nail as before.

ops.title: Attach the base
ops.body: Glue and nail the base across the bottom of the branch rows, orienting it so its longer edge faces down and its two 25-degree ends splay outward, matching the taper of the branches above — the base is the one piece whose ends are deliberately NOT parallel. Line its centerline up with the peak so the tree reads straight. Stand it on a flat surface and confirm it sits upright before the glue sets.

ops.title: Add the trunk
ops.body: Glue and nail the trunk block centered under the base — 2 3/4" for the small tree, 4 3/4" for the medium, 6 3/4" for the large — running vertically so the tree stands on the trunk's end grain. Center it against the base's midpoint; an off-center trunk makes the whole tree look like it is leaning. Repeat the whole process for each size — built to these lengths the three nest inside one another and stack away together after the holidays.

baseStepTitles: Add the first row of branches
Add the second row of branches

flags: SYSTEMIC GAP: no step produced the parts. A cut step was inserted at index 0.

DERIVED and now stated: the finished circle is 31 1/2" across, so the string radius is 15 3/4". Eight 3 1/2" boards plus seven 1/2" gaps = 31 1/2", which equals the longest tabletop board — the cut list is self-consistent with a 31 1/2" circle, and the other three pair lengths check out as chords of it (30 7/16 vs a computed 30.33, 27 1/16 vs 26.83, 20 vs 19.80 — each cut slightly long so there is material to cut back to the line). The source never states a diameter or a radius anywhere.
Decimal cut-list lengths converted to tape-measure fractions: 30.4375 = 30 7/16", 27.0625 = 27 1/16", 29.75 = 29 3/4", 23.5 = 23 1/2", 17.5 = 17 1/2".

Lumber verified with packBoards: 1x4 parts at 120" boards = 2 boards against 2 supplied (exact); 1x2 parts at 96" boards = 3 against 3 supplied (exact, worst slack 2 1/8"). No shortfall, but no spare either.

UNDER-SPECIFIED IN THE SOURCE, not invented: the X legs' spread, how far apart the two leg supports sit, and which leg of each pair crosses in front. The rewrite gives the joint and the fold logic and stops there.
DERIVED: the 3 1/2" spacers pack the leg supports out so the two legs of a pair sit in different planes and can cross. The cut list gives the part but no stated function.
run1-box-geometry.mjs returned no determination for this plan.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: From the two 10 ft cedar 1x4s cut the eight tabletop boards in four matched pairs: two at 31 1/2", two at 30 7/16", two at 27 1/16" and two at 20". Those four lengths are the chords of a 31 1/2" circle, which is why they are odd numbers — cut them accurately and keep each pair together. From the three 1x2s cut two tabletop supports at 29 3/4", two leg supports at 23 1/2", two spacers at 3 1/2", four legs at 30" with both ends cut at 45 degrees and parallel to each other, and two base support stretchers at 17 1/2". Cut the four legs first with the saw set once so they come out identical; they are the longest and the only angled parts, and a leg even slightly off will make the X sit crooked. Both 1x4s and all three 1x2s are used nearly to the end, so there is no spare for a miscut.

ops.title: Build and cut the round tabletop
ops.body: Lay the eight boards out symmetrically about the center — the two 31 1/2" boards in the middle, then the 30 7/16" pair, the 27 1/16" pair, and the 20" pair on the outside — leaving a 1/2" gap between each. Eight 3 1/2" boards plus seven 1/2" gaps come to 31 1/2" across, the same as the longest board, so check that measurement before you fasten anything: it is the plan telling you the layout is right. Working outward from the center, screw the two 29 3/4" tabletop supports across the underside with 1 1/4" screws and glue. Then pin a string at the exact center of the assembly, tie a pencil at 15 3/4" — half of 31 1/2" — and swing the full circle, cut it freehand with the jigsaw staying just outside the line, and sand the edge fair. The circle cannot fix a crooked layout underneath it, so square everything up before the pencil comes out.

ops.title: Attach the leg supports
ops.body: Round the corners of the two 23 1/2" leg supports on the sander before they go on — they sit at shin height under an outdoor table and a square corner finds a leg. Lay them on the underside of the finished top and attach with 2" screws and glue. Keep them parallel to each other and set the same distance either side of center: the X legs hang off these, so any offset here shows up as a lean in the finished table.

ops.title: Add the spacers
ops.body: Round the corners of the two 3 1/2" spacers and attach them to the leg supports with 1 1/4" fasteners and glue. Their job is to pack the support out by the thickness of a 1x2 so the two legs of each pair end up in different planes and can cross past one another — without them the legs would collide at the top. Keep the glue off the faces the legs will pivot against if you are building the collapsible version.

ops.title: Attach the legs
ops.body: Each leg is cut at 45 degrees at both ends with the two cuts parallel, so when the leg leans at 45 degrees the top cut sits flat against the leg support and the bottom cut sits flat on the ground — set them that way and the table stands level without any trimming. Fasten each leg at the top through the leg support and its spacer. Use a single fastener per top joint rather than two: a second screw locks the joint and kills the fold. For a collapsible table, put a removable bolt here instead of a screw.

ops.title: Connect the legs at center
ops.body: Stand the table up, let the legs settle into the spread you want, and fasten the crossing pair to each other at the point where they overlap — again with one fastener, which is the pivot the whole X turns on. Check the top is level and the legs are splayed the same amount on both sides before you drive it. For a collapsible table use a removable bolt here as well; these two joints and the leg tops are the only things holding the X, so they take the load either way.

ops.title: Add the base supports
ops.body: Attach the two 17 1/2" base support stretchers between the legs near the bottom, one per side, with the legs standing at their finished spread. Measure the spread at the top and set the bottom to match so the X stays symmetrical rather than leaning. These stretchers are what stop the base walking sideways when someone sets a drink down. Fasten with the exterior screws and skip the glue here — if the table is meant to collapse, this is the piece that comes off, so you want fasteners you can back out cleanly.

baseStepTitles: Build and cut the round tabletop

flags: SYSTEMIC GAP: no step produced the parts. A cut step was inserted at index 0.

run1-box-geometry.mjs DETERMINATION, used verbatim in the drawer step: 'Large drawer sides (16") runs the FULL length and Large drawer front/back (23-3/4") sits BETWEEN them — proved by the Drawer bottoms at 16" x 25-1/4".' Same reading for the top drawer. So each box is 16" deep and 23 3/4 + 1 1/2 = 25 1/4" wide, exactly the bottoms.

Arithmetic checks that CLOSE and are now stated: the 32" back supports between two 3/4" side panels give 33 1/2" overall, and the face frame is 26 1/2" rails plus two 3 1/2" front legs = 33 1/2" — the two agree exactly. Three 5 1/2" boards give a 16 1/2" deep side panel, and the 18" side trim is that depth plus the 3/4" overhang at each end the source already calls for. A 25 1/4" drawer box in the 26 1/2" face frame opening leaves 5/8" per side for the euro slides.

DERIVED: the top drawer is the shallow one (3 1/2" sides from 1x4) over two 5 1/2" boxes (1x6). The cut list gives the two heights but never says which opening is which; the material split is what determines it.
Lumber verified with packBoards at 96" boards: 1x6 = 5 against 5 supplied but with only 1/2" of worst-case slack — no room for a miscut, stated in the prose. 1x2 = 3 of 3, 1x3 = 2 of 2, 1x4 = 2 of 2.

The vanity top has NO parts in the cut list (deliberate per the description — it is built around your own sink), and the 1x4 allowance is fully consumed by the front legs and top drawer, so top material has to be bought separately. Stated in the cut step; no top dimension invented.
Drawer slide height and setback are left to the hardware's own template rather than given a number, since the source does not specify them.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: From the 1x6s cut six side panel boards and two back panel boards at 31 3/4", four large drawer sides at 16", and four large drawer front/back at 23 3/4" — that fills all five 8 ft boards with about half an inch of slack on the tightest one, so measure twice before every cut. From the 1x4s cut two front legs at 31 3/4", two top drawer sides at 16", and two top drawer front/back at 23 3/4". From the 1x3s cut two back supports at 32" and two face frame top/bottom rails at 26 1/2"; from the 1x2s cut four side trim at 18", two face frame middle rails at 26 1/2", two drawer slide supports at 31 3/4", and two front trim at 35" left long to trim to fit later. Cut three drawer bottoms 16" x 25 1/4" from the 1/4" plywood. The cut list carries no parts for the top and the 1x4 allowance will not cover one either — that is deliberate, since the top is built around your own sink, so buy that material separately once you have chosen it.

ops.title: Build the side panels
ops.body: Glue three 31 3/4" 1x6 boards edge to edge on a flat surface for each side panel — three 5 1/2" boards give a side 16 1/2" deep and 31 3/4" tall. Clamp across the joints and check the panel is flat before the glue sets; a cupped side panel will never let the face frame sit square. Attach the 18" 1x2 trim on top, which is that 16 1/2" depth plus the 3/4" of overhang at each end. Leave a 2 1/2" space under the bottom trim.

ops.title: Attach the back supports
ops.body: Drill two pocket holes at each end of the two 32" back support boards and screw them BETWEEN the two side panels, with the pocket holes exposed on the back where nobody sees them. This is what sets the cabinet's width: 32" plus the two 3/4" sides is 33 1/2", which is exactly the face frame you build next — 26 1/2" rails plus two 3 1/2" front legs. Set one support at the top and one at the bottom, flush with the back edges of the sides. Compare the diagonals now, because everything downstream is squared to this box.

ops.title: Build and attach the face frame
ops.body: Build the frame flat: the two 31 3/4" 1x4 front legs run the full height, and the 26 1/2" rails fit between them — two 1x3 at the top and bottom, two 1x2 in the middle — giving a 33 1/2" wide frame that matches the carcass exactly. Space the middle rails so the openings suit the drawer boxes you will build: one shallow opening at the top for the 3 1/2" tall 1x4 box, and two deeper ones below for the 5 1/2" tall 1x6 boxes. Join it with pocket holes and 1 1/4" pocket hole screws, then nail and glue the frame to the front of the cabinet. Check for square by comparing diagonal measurements before moving on.

ops.title: Build the drawers
ops.body: Build all three boxes 16" deep: the 16" sides run the FULL depth and the fronts and backs fit BETWEEN them, so at 23 3/4" each box comes out 25 1/4" wide overall — exactly the 16" x 25 1/4" plywood bottoms, which is your check that you have it the right way round. Two boxes are 5 1/2" tall out of 1x6 and the top one is 3 1/2" tall out of 1x4. Join the corners with 3/4" pocket holes and 1 1/4" pocket hole screws, then nail and glue a plywood bottom onto each. Where a drawer has to clear plumbing, keep these same cuts but box out the corner and set the back forward of the pipe rather than resizing the box.

ops.title: Install the drawer slides
ops.body: A 25 1/4" box in the 26 1/2" face frame opening leaves about 5/8" per side, which is what the 16" euro slides are meant to take up — confirm that against your own hardware before you drill anything, because brands differ. Mount the front of each slide to the 1x2 drawer slide support and the back into a rear end socket screwed to the back panel boards, which is why those boards went on before this step. Follow the slide's own template for height and setback rather than a number from this plan, and set the two sides of each pair at exactly the same height or the drawer will bind.
materials: Euro style drawer slides, 16 in, pair

baseStepTitles: Build and attach the face frame
